serving.natural_language_portion: One mini serving of Thai beef boat noodles soup is approximately 350 g, comprising a small bowl of rice noodles in spiced beef broth with beef meatballs, vegetables, and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Mini Thai Beef Boat Noodles (Soup) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mini Thai Beef Boat Noodles (Soup), CR ID F05060087. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles (kway teow) — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai beef boat noodles soup as a composite dish. GI estimated at 53 (low-medium boundary) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: rice noodles (kway teow), which have a published GI of approximately 40–61 in the University of Sydney GI Database depending on preparation. A midpoint of 53 is used. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (53) × available carbohydrate per serving (9.6 g/100g × 350 g serving = 33.6 g) ÷ 100 = 17.8 g GL. The broth-based preparation and protein content (3.9 g/100g) are expected to moderate the glycaemic response relative to dry noodle preparations.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 554 mg per 100 g — borderline-to-high sodium load with no significant potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce, and consume the broth sparingly or on the side, as the 554 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,939 mg per 350 g serving) substantially exceeds the recommended 600 mg per meal threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (9.6 g/100g) from rice noodles with moderate-to-high GI; protein content partially moderates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit noodle portion, increase the vegetable proportion, avoid adding sweet condiments such as hoisin sauce, and monitor post-meal blood glucose, as the rice noodle base carries a GI of approximately 53 and a glycaemic load of ~17.8 per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (554 mg/100g) and presence of saturated fat from beef; low total fat overall but sodium is the primary cardiovascular risk driver
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional food due to its high sodium content (approximately 1,939 mg per 350 g serving), request reduced seasoning, limit broth consumption, and prefer leaner protein substitutions where possible to reduce saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef (arachidonic acid source), high sodium, and presence of nightshade ingredients (chilli)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of consumption due to beef's pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content, the high sodium load worsening tissue swelling, and the presence of chilli (nightshade) which may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mini Thai Beef Boat Noodles Soup (ก๋วยเตี๋ยวเรือเนื้อไทยมินิ, น้ำ) is a Thai-origin broth-based noodle dish served in Singapore as a mini portion. It consists of thin rice noodles (kway teow) submerged in a richly spiced beef broth seasoned with fish sauce, soy sauce, cinnamon, and star anise, topped with mini beef meatballs, sliced beef, bean sprouts, morning glory (water spinach), Thai basil, and chilli. The dish is characterised by a savoury, slightly sweet, and mildly spiced flavour profile with a light but aromatic broth. Texturally, the noodles are soft and silky, the meatballs are tender, and the vegetables provide a slight crunch. Per 100 g, the dish provides 71 kcal, 3.9 g protein, 1.85 g fat, and 9.6 g carbohydrate, making it a relatively low-calorie, moderate-carbohydrate option. Sodium is notably high at 554 mg per 100 g, which is a primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The GI is estimated at approximately 53 (low-medium) based on the rice noodle component. This dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. It is a popular hawker-style dish in Singapore, commonly consumed at breakfast or lunch, and is widely available at Thai food stalls and hawker centres.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05060087) at confidence 0.95. The food identity is exact. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database, rice noodles) and is clearly documented as an analogous estimate. Multilingual names are provided for Thai (mandatory), English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi (Singapore context). Ontology anchors are approximate but defensible.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (F05060087) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g is estimated at 0.7 g/100g based on typical beef meatball and broth composition from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (beef meatball soup analogue) as HPB does not report this field separately; this is an adapted estimate. Input cultural tags 'western', 'vegetarian', 'plant_based', 'wholegrain' were assessed as incorrect for this dish and have been corrected: the dish is Thai cuisine, contains beef, and is not plant-based or wholegrain. Halal status is not confirmed for this generic record as it contains beef (source of halal concern depending on slaughter method); is_halal set to false pending confirmation. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis referenced trans fat of ~0.1 g/100g; this value is not confirmed in the HPB source and has been set to -1 pending verification.
